title: From Sovereigns to Servants. How the War Against Ukraine Reshaped Russia’s Elite
gist: Carnegie's Alexandra Prokopenko argues that Western sanctions failed to turn Russia's liberal and business elite against Putin; instead the elite rewired the economy for war and lost its agency, becoming an instrument of governance rather than an independent power class.

themes.theme: Russian elite co-optation and loss of agency

insights.claim: Contrary to Western expectations that crushing sanctions would push Russia's liberal officials and global businessmen to force Putin to stop the war, those elites instead rewired the economy for war, with only a handful resigning or speaking out.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Undercuts the thesis that sanctions can trigger elite-driven regime pressure; implies sanctions are unlikely to end the war via internal Russian dissent.
insights.quote: Instead, liberal-minded officials rewired the economy for war; corporations obeyed new rules; and only a handful resigned or spoke out.
insights.locator: When Moscow launched its full-scale invasion

insights.claim: Prokopenko argues Russia's upper class retained the formal attributes of power but lost its agency, essentially becoming a mere instrument of governance.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Suggests no meaningful internal counterweight to Putin exists, lengthening the likely duration of the conflict and sanctions regime for investors with Russia/EM exposure.
insights.quote: Though the Russian upper class retained formal attributes of power, in reality it lost its agency, essentially becoming a mere instrument of governance.
insights.locator: Built on dozens of candid conversations

insights.claim: The book traces how competing Russian factions, from technocrats to hawks, rationalised shocks such as Western sanctions, mass mobilisation, the annexation of four Ukrainian regions, and Yevgeny Prigozhin's aborted 2023 mutiny, recalibrating their loyalties at each stage.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Provides a framework for reading future Russian shocks; elite moral boundaries expanding to match superiors' expectations means watch leadership signals, not elite defections.
insights.quote: She traces meticulously how competing factions, from technocrats to hawks, rationalised each shock and recalibrated their loyalties.
insights.locator: Alexandra Prokopenko illuminates the war’s pivotal moments
